claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have one child, Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 16 years old, I need to ask: Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex actually just left school recently and has signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know. 

Can you tell me when Alex registered with the careers service? Specifically, has it been within the last 20 weeks since leaving education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been within the last 20 weeks since leaving school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 16 years old
- Alex lives with you in the UK
- Alex recently left school and registered with a government-sponsored careers service
- This registration happened within the last 20 weeks

According to the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Even though Alex has left school, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks because Alex is 16 years old and has registered with the careers service. This is a special provision that allows Child Benefit to continue while young people between 16-17 years old figure out their next steps after leaving education.

Please note that this eligibility will last for the 20-week period from when Alex registered with the careers service. After that, your eligibility would depend on whether Alex starts approved education, training, or other qualifying circumstances.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. Although Alex has left school, they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service within the last 20 weeks. According to the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex is within this 20-week window, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has left school, they registered with the careers service within the last 20 weeks, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks under the special provision for 16-17 year olds who leave education and register with a careers service.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 16 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. Although Alex has left school, they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service within the last 20 weeks. According to the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex is within this 20-week window, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has left school, they registered with the careers service within the last 20 weeks, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks under the special provision for 16-17 year olds who leave education and register with a careers service.
